title: ELECTRA: Pre-training Text Encoders as Discriminators Rather Than Generators
authors: Kevin Clark, Minh-Thang Luong, Quoc V. Le, Christopher D. Manning
hook: What if language models learned by catching fakes instead of filling in blanks?
promise: You will understand how ELECTRA trains language models more efficiently by detecting replaced words.
prerequisite_note: It helps to grasp how models learn from predicting missing or altered words in sentences.

glossary.plain: Training a model on a general task before fine-tuning for specific applications.
A technique where some words are hidden and the model learns to guess them.
A small model that proposes plausible words to replace in the input text.
A model trained to decide if each word in a sentence is real or has been replaced.
How much useful learning a model achieves from a given amount of data or compute.
The task of identifying which words in a sentence have been substituted by the generator.

cards.title: Spot the Fake, Learn Faster
body: See how ELECTRA trains language models to catch replaced words for rapid learning.

cards.title: Why just fill in the blanks?
body: Imagine teaching a language model by hiding words and asking it to guess them. This is how many models, like BERT, learn about language.
But what if instead, we train the model to spot words that do not belong? ELECTRA flips the script and finds a more efficient way to learn.

cards.title: From guessing to detecting
body: Traditional models mask a few words and train the model to predict them, focusing only on these masked spots.
ELECTRA replaces some words with plausible alternatives, then trains a model to judge every word, asking if it is original or swapped.
cards.right: Discriminator predicts which words are fake
cards.caption: ELECTRA involves both a generator that replaces words and a discriminator that spots them.

cards.title: Guess vs Detect: Which is smarter?
body: Toggle between the classic masked language modeling task and ELECTRA's replaced token detection.
cards.offNote: Only a few words teach the model at once, wasting potential learning.
cards.onNote: Every word in the sentence becomes a learning opportunity, making training more efficient.

cards.title: The replaced token trick
body: Instead of hiding words, ELECTRA replaces some words with others suggested by a smaller generator model, making the input look plausible but subtly altered.
The main model, called the discriminator, must decide for each word whether it is original or swapped, forcing it to pay attention to every word.

cards.title: Adjust the percent of replaced words
body: See how changing the amount of replaced words affects learning and realism.
cards.low: Too few replaced words and the task is too easy, so little is learned.
cards.mid: A balanced amount keeps the task challenging and the sentences natural.
cards.high: Too many fakes and sentences become unnatural, which confuses the model.

cards.title: What goes into ELECTRA?
body: Pick the parts that make the ELECTRA pretraining process unique.

choices.label: Replaced token detection
choices.note: Main training task for the discriminator.

choices.label: Discriminator
choices.note: Learns to spot replaced words in the sentence.

choices.label: All-token supervision
choices.note: Every word provides a learning signal.

choices.label: Efficient use of compute
choices.note: Learns more per training step than MLM.

cards.title: ELECTRA's impact on model efficiency
body: ELECTRA models outperform BERT and even rival RoBERTa and XLNet while using less compute, especially for smaller models.
On the GLUE benchmark, ELECTRA trained with less hardware matches or beats models trained with much more data and time.

bars.label: GLUE score (small model)
bars.note: ELECTRA outperforms GPT with 30x less compute

bars.label: Compute needed (relative)
bars.note: Fraction of compute versus RoBERTa or XLNet

bars.label: All-token supervision
bars.note: ELECTRA uses every word for training

cards.caption: ELECTRA achieves strong results with less compute by learning from every word.

cards.title: Why ELECTRA changed pretraining
body: ELECTRA showed that language models can learn more efficiently by focusing on detecting subtle changes, not just filling in blanks.
Its approach inspired new pretraining strategies and proved that clever learning tasks can reduce the need for massive compute.

recap.label: Replace
recap.note: A generator swaps some words for plausible alternatives.

recap.label: Detect
recap.note: The discriminator decides if each word is original or fake.

recap.label: Learn
recap.note: The model improves by considering every word, not just masked ones.

recap.label: Transfer
recap.note: Resulting representations perform well on real language tasks.

cards.title: ELECTRA: Pre-training Text Encoders as Discriminators Rather Than Generators
body: Explore the full details and results in the original ELECTRA paper from 2020.
